Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)

Interactive Hematology Reference — PGY-1 Level

Peripheral smear: schistocytes (arrows) — hallmark of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (Wikimedia Commons)
Pathophysiology Clinical Labs PLASMIC Score Differential Management Monitoring Special Populations Pearls
1

Pathophysiology

ADAMTS13 deficiency leads to ultra-large vWF multimer accumulation, spontaneous platelet adhesion, microthrombi, and mechanical RBC shearing.
ADAMTS13 <10% UL-vWF Multimers Platelet-Rich Microthrombi Untreated: >90% mortality

ADAMTS13: The Central Enzyme

ADAMTS13 is a zinc-containing metalloprotease synthesized primarily by hepatic stellate cells, with minor contributions from endothelial cells, podocytes, and megakaryocytes.

  • Sole substrate: von Willebrand Factor (vWF)
  • Cleavage site: Tyr1605–Met1606 in the A2 domain of UL-vWF
  • Normal activity: 50–150% (0.5–1.5 IU/mL)
  • Trigger: High shear stress exposes the A2 domain for cleavage
Without ADAMTS13 cleavage, UL-vWF multimers remain anchored to endothelium and unfurl into flowing blood, creating massive strings that spontaneously capture platelets even without vascular injury.

Congenital TTP (Upshaw-Schulman Syndrome)

  • Frequency: ~5% of all TTP cases
  • Genetics: Biallelic loss-of-function mutations in ADAMTS13 gene (chromosome 9q34)
  • Inheritance: Autosomal recessive (>200 mutations identified)
  • ADAMTS13 activity: Constitutively <5–10%, no inhibitory antibodies
  • Presentation: Variable — neonatal hemolytic anemia to adult-onset during pregnancy/stress
  • Heterozygous carriers: ADAMTS13 40–70%, asymptomatic
Pregnancy is a common trigger for congenital TTP because vWF levels physiologically rise 2–3x during gestation, overwhelming the already-deficient ADAMTS13.

Acquired TTP: Autoimmune Destruction (>95% of cases)

Caused by IgG autoantibodies (predominantly IgG4 and IgG1) against ADAMTS13.

Antibody Types

  • Neutralizing: Directly block catalytic activity ("inhibitors") — measured in Bethesda units
  • Non-neutralizing: Accelerate plasma clearance of ADAMTS13
  • Inhibitor >2 BU: High burden → higher relapse risk, potential refractory disease

Triggers & Associations

CategoryExamples
InfectionsHIV, hepatitis C, H. pylori, CMV
Pregnancy2nd/3rd trimester, postpartum
AutoimmuneSLE (10–15%), antiphospholipid syndrome, RA
DrugsTiclopidine, clopidogrel, quinine, calcineurin inhibitors
MalignancyVarious
TransplantBMT, solid organ
IdiopathicMany cases — no trigger identified

The Microvascular Thrombosis Cascade

When ADAMTS13 is deficient, UL-vWF multimers (>20,000 kDa) persist on endothelium. Under high shear stress (>5,000/s), they spontaneously bind platelet GPIb-IX-V receptors.

Key Distinctions

  • "White clots" (platelet-rich) — distinct from "red clots" (fibrin-rich) of DIC
  • Coagulation cascade NOT activated → PT, PTT, fibrinogen all NORMAL
  • Platelet consumption is mechanical/thrombotic — not immune destruction (unlike ITP)
Platelet transfusion = "fuel to the fire." Adding platelets provides more substrate for microthrombi, worsening organ damage and potentially causing death.

End-Organ Damage & Hemolysis

Microthrombi act as a mechanical sieve. RBCs forced through narrowed microvasculature are sheared into schistocytes. This is MAHA: mechanical, non-immune (Coombs-negative).

OrganManifestationNotes
BrainFluctuating neuro symptoms, seizures, stroke, comaPreferentially affected; waxing/waning is classic
KidneysMild AKI (Cr 1.5–2.5)Less severe than HUS
HeartTroponin elevation, arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy, sudden deathUnderappreciated cause of mortality
GIAbdominal pain, pancreatitisMesenteric/pancreatic microthrombi
AdrenalsAdrenal insufficiency (rare)Microthrombi in adrenal vasculature
2

Clinical Presentation

Classic pentad present in <5% at diagnosis. Modern dx: thrombocytopenia + MAHA + no alternative explanation.
Pentad <5% Fluctuating Neuro F:M 3:1
Schistocytes (marked) in TTP (Wikimedia Commons)
Additional schistocyte examples (Wikimedia Commons)
RBC morphology reference (Wikimedia Commons)

The Classic Pentad (Historical — Present in <5%)

ComponentFrequencyKey Detail
1. Thrombocytopenia>95%Median ~10,000–20,000/mcL, almost always <30,000
2. MAHA>95%Schistocytes >1%, LDH often >1,000, haptoglobin undetectable
3. Neurological60–70%Headache, confusion, focal deficits, seizures (10–20%), fluctuating
4. Renal~50%Mild: Cr 1.5–2.5. If Cr >3–4 or dialysis → think HUS
5. Fever~25%Low-grade (37.8–38.5°C), non-specific
Do NOT wait for all five features. Thrombocytopenia + MAHA without alternative explanation = start TPE immediately.

Detailed Symptom Breakdown

FindingFrequencyDetails
Cardiac25–50%Troponin elevation, chest pain, arrhythmias, HF, sudden cardiac death. ECG: ST changes, QTc prolongation.
Abdominal30–40%N/V, abdominal pain, diarrhea. Pancreatitis (lipase elevation) in 15–20%.
Skin40–50%Petechiae, purpura, ecchymoses (thrombocytopenia). Jaundice (hemolysis).
ConstitutionalCommonFatigue, malaise, weakness — often initial symptoms.
Fluctuating neurological symptoms are nearly pathognomonic. Confused one hour, lucid the next, then seizes = TTP until proven otherwise. Reflects dynamic microthrombi formation and resolution.

Epidemiology (Clinically Pertinent)

ParameterValue
Incidence~3–6 per million/year
SexFemale predominance 3:1
Peak age30–50 years (acquired); neonatal/childhood (congenital)
Racial disparityAfrican Americans: ~9x higher risk
Untreated mortality>90%
Modern quad-therapy<5% mortality
Relapse rate30–50%, highest in first 6 months to 2 years
HIV-associated30–40x higher incidence
Pregnancy-associated~10–25% of TTP episodes
3

Lab Findings

Normal coags (PT/PTT/fibrinogen) with thrombocytopenia + MAHA = TTP, not DIC. Draw ADAMTS13 before plasma exchange.
Schistocytes LDH >1000 Coombs Negative Normal Coags
NORMAL PT / PTT / FIBRINOGEN = NOT DIC — The single most important lab distinction and the most tested point.

Expected Lab Values in TTP

TestExpectedSignificance
Platelet count<30,000/mcL (median ~15,000)Consumption into microthrombi
Hemoglobin6–9 g/dLMAHA
WBCNormal or mildly elevatedHelps rule out leukemia/aplastic anemia
Peripheral smearSchistocytes (>1%, often 2–5%+), polychromasia, nRBCsMANDATORY — do not diagnose without it
LDHOften >1,000 U/L (sometimes >2,000)Hemolysis + tissue ischemia. Best daily marker.
HaptoglobinUsually undetectable (<10 mg/dL)Consumed binding free Hgb
Indirect bilirubin2–5 mg/dLHemolysis byproduct
Reticulocyte count>4–6% (absolute >120K/mcL)Appropriate marrow response
CreatinineMild: 1.5–2.5 mg/dLIf >3–4 → think HUS
PT / PTTNORMALKEY: distinguishes from DIC
FibrinogenNORMAL (often elevated as APR)KEY: low in DIC, normal in TTP
D-dimerNormal or mildly elevatedNot as dramatic as DIC
Direct CoombsNegativeRules out AIHA — mechanical hemolysis
ADAMTS13 activity<10% = diagnosticSEND BEFORE PLASMA EXCHANGE
ADAMTS13 inhibitorPositive in acquired TTP (Bethesda units)>2 BU = high inhibitor burden
Anti-ADAMTS13 IgGPositive in acquired TTPMore sensitive than functional inhibitor
TroponinElevated in 25–50%Check on ALL TTP patients
LipaseMay be elevatedPancreatic microthrombi

Critical Lab Interpretation Rules

1Normal PT/PTT/fibrinogen = NOT DIC. Coagulation cascade is not activated in TTP — only the platelet plug pathway is dysfunctional.
2Negative Coombs = NOT AIHA. TTP is mechanical hemolysis (shearing through platelet/vWF mesh), not antibody-mediated.
3Draw ADAMTS13 BEFORE plasma exchange. TPE normalizes ADAMTS13 levels, making the diagnostic test uninterpretable. Common exam pitfall.
4LDH is the best daily monitoring marker — reflects both hemolysis and tissue ischemia, responds faster than platelet count.
5If Cr >3–4 mg/dL or dialysis required → strongly reconsider TTP. Think HUS or aHUS instead.
4

PLASMIC Score Calculator

Predicts likelihood of severe ADAMTS13 deficiency (<10%). Guides empiric TPE initiation while awaiting ADAMTS13 results (2–5 day turnaround).
Score 6–7 = Start TPE PPV ~70%

PLASMIC Score — Click Each Criterion Present

P Platelet count <30 x 10⁹/L
L Combined hemoLysis variable — retic >2.5% OR haptoglobin undetectable OR indirect bili >2.0
A No Active cancer in past year
S No Stem cell or solid organ transplant history
M MCV <90 fL
I INR <1.5
C Creatinine <2.0 mg/dL
0
Select criteria above

Score Interpretation

ScoreRiskP(ADAMTS13 <10%)Action
0–4Low<5%TTP unlikely; investigate other TMA causes
5Intermediate~10–25%Clinical judgment; consider empiric TPE if high suspicion
6–7High~62–72%Start TPE empirically while awaiting ADAMTS13
MCV <90 excludes B12/folate deficiency mimics. INR <1.5 helps exclude DIC. Creatinine <2.0 helps exclude HUS.
5

Differential Diagnosis

TTP must be distinguished from other thrombotic microangiopathies and causes of thrombocytopenia with hemolysis.
TTP vs HUS vs DIC ADAMTS13 is Key

Comprehensive TMA Comparison

Feature TTP STEC-HUS aHUS DIC ITP HELLP Evans
MechanismADAMTS13 deficiencyShiga toxinComplement dysregSystemic coag activationAntiplatelet AbPregnancy endothelialAIHA + ITP
ADAMTS13<10%NormalNormalNormal/mild lowNormalUsually >10%Normal
Platelets<30K (severe)20–100K20–100KVariableVariableUsually >20KLow
SchistocytesYes (prominent)YesYesYes (variable)NoYesNo
PT/PTTNORMALNormalNormalABNORMALNormalNormal/mildNormal
FibrinogenNORMAL/highNormalNormalLOWNormalLow-normalNormal
RenalMild (Cr 1.5–2.5)SEVERE (dialysis)SEVEREVariableNoneMild-modNone
NeuroPROMINENT (60–70%)Rare (<10%)~20%VariableNonePossibleNone
CoombsNegativeNegativeNegativeNegativeNegativeNegativePOSITIVE
Key ClueFluctuating neuro, ADAMTS13<10%Bloody diarrhea, E.coli O157, kidsComplement mutations, recurrentCoagulopathy + trigger (sepsis)Isolated low plt, no MAHAPregnancy >20wk, high LFTsDAT+, dual cytopenia
TreatmentTPE + steroids + caplacizumabSupportive (NO abx for E.coli)Eculizumab/ravulizumabTreat underlying causeSteroids, IVIG, rituximabDeliverySteroids, rituximab

Pregnancy-Associated TMA Distinction

FeatureTTPHELLPPreeclampsiaaHUS
TimingAny trimester, postpartum>20 weeks, postpartum>20 weeksUsually postpartum
ADAMTS13<10%>10% (usually >30%)NormalNormal
Liver enzymesNormal/mildMarkedly elevatedMildNormal
HypertensionAbsent or mildPresentDefining featureMay be present
Response to deliveryNO improvementImproves 48–72hImproves 48–72hNO improvement
TreatmentTPEDeliveryDelivery + MgEculizumab
ADAMTS13 <10% = TTP, not HELLP. HELLP/preeclampsia improve after delivery; TTP does not. This is the key diagnostic and therapeutic distinction in pregnancy-associated TMA.
6

Management

Hematologic emergency requiring same-day TPE. Modern quad-therapy: TPE + steroids + caplacizumab + rituximab reduces mortality to <5%.
TPE = Cornerstone NO Platelet Transfusion Untreated: >90% mortality
Plasmapheresis setup for therapeutic plasma exchange (Wikimedia Commons)

Immediate Actions (Within Hours)

  1. DO NOT WAIT for ADAMTS13 results — this is a hematologic emergency
  2. Draw ADAMTS13 activity + inhibitor level BEFORE any plasma exchange or infusion
  3. DO NOT TRANSFUSE PLATELETS — provides substrate for microthrombi, can cause death
  4. Consult hematology STAT + contact blood bank/apheresis for urgent TPE
  5. Supportive care: folate 1 mg daily, RBC transfusion PRN (safe, unlike platelets), hold VTE ppx
Only exceptions for platelet transfusion: life-threatening hemorrhage (active ICH, massive GI bleed with hemodynamic instability) or required for central line placement for TPE catheter.

Plasma Exchange (TPE) — The Cornerstone

Without TPE, mortality is >90%.

ParameterDetail
Mechanism(1) Removes anti-ADAMTS13 autoantibodies (2) Removes UL-vWF multimers (3) Replaces functional ADAMTS13
Dose1.0–1.5 plasma volumes/session (40–60 mL/kg). 1.5x for severe patients.
FrequencyDaily until response
Replacement fluidFFP or cryo-poor plasma. NOT albumin (contains no ADAMTS13).
AccessDouble-lumen dialysis catheter (IJ or femoral). Avoid subclavian (non-compressible + thrombocytopenia).
Response criteriaPlatelets >150K for 2 consecutive days + normalizing LDH + clinical improvement
TaperAfter response: every other day x 2–3 sessions, then stop.
TimelinePlatelets rise in 2–3 days. Median remission: 7–9 days. No response by day 5–7 = refractory.
Bridge if unavailableFFP infusion 15–30 mL/kg (provides ADAMTS13 but does NOT remove antibodies)

TPE Complications

  • Citrate toxicity: chelates Ca2+ → perioral/extremity paresthesias, cramps, QTc prolongation. Prophylax with calcium gluconate IV.
  • Catheter-related: infection (4–8%), thrombosis, pneumothorax, hemorrhage
  • Allergic reactions: urticaria, anaphylaxis. Pre-medicate: diphenhydramine + acetaminophen.
  • Hypotension / Hypothermia from volume shifts and cold plasma

Corticosteroids

ParameterDetail
TimingStart concurrently with first TPE
StandardPrednisone 1 mg/kg/day PO
Pulse (severe)Methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily x 3 days, then prednisone 1 mg/kg/day
MechanismSuppresses autoantibody production; anti-inflammatory on endothelium
DurationThrough acute treatment, then taper over 2–4 weeks
MonitoringBlood glucose, GI prophylaxis (PPI), infection risk

Caplacizumab (Cablivi)

ParameterDetail
ClassAnti-vWF nanobody (bivalent single-domain antibody fragment)
MechanismBinds vWF A1 domain, blocks vWF-platelet GPIb interaction. Immediately prevents new microthrombus formation.
Dosing11 mg IV bolus pre-TPE, then 11 mg SubQ daily (30 min post-TPE) through course + 30 days after last TPE
Key trialHERCULES (2019 NEJM): 74% reduction in composite endpoint. Faster platelet normalization. No TTP deaths in treatment arm.
GuidelinesASH 2020 + ISTH 2020: first-line for acquired TTP
Side effectsIncreased bleeding risk (epistaxis, gingival, bruising). Headache.
Cost~$270,000/course (cost-effective given reduced ICU stays/mortality)
MonitoringIf ADAMTS13 <10% when stopped → restart + add rituximab

Rituximab (Rituxan)

ParameterDetail
ClassAnti-CD20 chimeric monoclonal antibody
MechanismDepletes CD20+ B cells (precursors of antibody-producing plasma cells). Full effect: 2–4 weeks.
Dosing375 mg/m² IV weekly x 4 doses
IndicationsRefractory, relapsing, high inhibitor (>2 BU), preemptive (ADAMTS13 <10% in remission), increasingly first-line
TPE timingGive AFTER TPE (not before) — TPE removes rituximab. Give on TPE-off days or 4+ hours post-TPE.
ResponseADAMTS13 recovery to >20% in 1–3 weeks. B cell depletion lasts 6–12 months.
Side effectsInfusion reactions, HBV reactivation (screen HBsAg/anti-HBc), PML (rare), hypogammaglobulinemia
Caplacizumab works immediately (blocks vWF-platelet binding) while rituximab takes weeks (B cell depletion). They are complementary, not redundant.

Refractory TTP

Definition: No platelet recovery (remains <50K or fails to double) after 5–7 days of daily TPE + steroids.

StepInterventionDetail
1Twice-daily TPE1.5 plasma volumes BID
2Rituximab375 mg/m² weekly x 4 (if not already started)
3CaplacizumabImmediate effect (if not already started)
4BortezomibProteasome inhibitor — targets CD20-negative plasma cells. 1.3 mg/m² SubQ days 1, 4, 8, 11.
5CyclosporineCalcineurin inhibitor. 2–3 mg/kg/day, trough 150–200 ng/mL.
6Vincristine1 mg IV weekly x 3–4 doses (rarely used)
7SplenectomyLast resort. High perioperative risk in active TTP.
8N-acetylcysteineExperimental — may reduce UL-vWF size. Limited evidence.
7

Monitoring & Follow-Up

Acute inpatient monitoring transitions to lifelong outpatient ADAMTS13 surveillance. Preemptive rituximab when ADAMTS13 <10% in remission prevents relapse.
LDH = Best Daily Marker Lifelong ADAMTS13 30–50% Relapse

Inpatient Monitoring (Acute Episode)

ParameterFrequencyTarget / Notes
Platelet countDaily (BID in ICU)Trending toward >150K for 2 consecutive days
LDHDailyMost sensitive marker; falling LDH before rising platelets = good early sign
HaptoglobinEvery 1–2 daysRecovery from undetectable = late response sign
CreatinineDailyShould remain stable or improve
TroponinAt dx, then q24–48h if elevatedCardiac ischemia monitoring
Neuro examEvery 8–12 hoursDocument fluctuations; improvement expected with TPE
ADAMTS13At dx (pre-TPE), q3–5d during TPE, at remissionConfirms dx; guides treatment duration
Ionized calciumDuring/after each TPECitrate toxicity monitoring

Remission Criteria

  • Platelet count >150,000/mcL for at least 2 consecutive days
  • LDH normalizing (clearly trending down)
  • Clinical improvement (neurological symptoms resolved)
  • No evidence of ongoing hemolysis

Outpatient Surveillance Schedule

TimeframeMonitoring
Weeks 1–4CBC + LDH + haptoglobin twice weekly. Taper steroids. Complete rituximab if started.
Months 1–3CBC + LDH + ADAMTS13 monthly
Months 3–24ADAMTS13 activity every 3 months (critical surveillance period)
After 2 yearsADAMTS13 every 6–12 months indefinitely (lifelong)

Relapse Prevention

  • ADAMTS13 <10% in remission (even asymptomatic) → preemptive rituximab (375 mg/m² weekly x 4). Proven to prevent clinical relapse.
  • ADAMTS13 10–20% → monitor monthly; consider preemptive rituximab if trending down.
  • Relapse rate: 30–50% within first 2 years, highest in first 6 months.

Relapse Risk Factors

  • High inhibitor titer at diagnosis
  • Persistent low ADAMTS13 during remission
  • Black race
  • Obesity
  • High anti-ADAMTS13 IgG levels

Pregnancy Counseling

  • High relapse risk (>25–50% per pregnancy)
  • Monitor ADAMTS13 monthly throughout (start preconception if planned)
  • Hematology co-management mandatory
  • If ADAMTS13 <10%: prophylactic plasma infusions or TPE
  • Preemptive rituximab before planned pregnancy (complete 6+ months before conception)
8

Special Populations

Pregnancy, HIV, drug-induced, and congenital TTP each have unique management considerations.
Pregnancy HIV Drug-Induced Congenital

Pregnancy-Associated TTP

  • May be initial presentation of acquired or congenital TTP
  • ADAMTS13 <10% confirms TTP (vs HELLP/preeclampsia/aHUS)
  • TPE is safe during pregnancy — treatment of choice
  • Caplacizumab: limited safety data; case-by-case
  • Rituximab: crosses placenta in 2nd/3rd trimester; generally avoided
Delivery does NOT resolve TTP (unlike HELLP/preeclampsia, which improve within 48–72h). Key diagnostic and therapeutic distinction.

HIV-Associated TTP

  • Incidence 30–40x higher than general population
  • More common with low CD4 but can occur at any stage
  • Treatment: standard TTP therapy PLUS initiation/optimization of ART
  • Rituximab: use cautiously (further immunosuppression)
  • Prognosis improved in ART era; relapse lower with controlled HIV

Drug-Induced TMA

DrugADAMTS13Treatment
Ticlopidine<10% (true TTP)TPE + stop drug
ClopidogrelMay be <10%TPE + stop drug
QuinineUsually normalStop drug; TPE if severe
Cyclosporine/TacrolimusNormalDose reduce/stop
GemcitabineNormalStop; eculizumab may help
Only ticlopidine (and rarely clopidogrel) causes true ADAMTS13-deficient TTP. Other drug TMAs have normal ADAMTS13.

Congenital TTP (Upshaw-Schulman)

  • ADAMTS13 constitutively <5–10% due to biallelic mutations
  • No autoantibodies — inhibitor and IgG negative
  • Treatment: FFP infusion 10–15 mL/kg q2–3 weeks (t1/2 = 2–3 days)
  • Plasma exchange NOT necessary (no antibodies to remove)
  • Prophylactic FFP lifelong; increase during triggers
  • Recombinant ADAMTS13 (TAK-755) in clinical trials
Congenital TTP = FFP infusions, NOT plasma exchange. No antibodies to remove. Board-relevant distinction.
9

Prognosis & Clinical Pearls

Modern quad-therapy has transformed TTP from >90% mortality to <5%. These pearls are board-relevant and clinically essential.
Board Buzzwords 15 High-Yield Pearls

Prognosis by Treatment Era

TreatmentMortality
No treatment (historical)>90%
Plasma infusion alone~50%
TPE + steroids10–20%
TPE + steroids + caplacizumab~5–10%
TPE + steroids + caplacizumab + rituximab<5%

Board-Relevant Buzzwords

Schistocytes / Helmet Cells ADAMTS13 <10% UL-vWF Multimers Plasma Exchange NO Platelet Transfusion Fluctuating Neuro Normal PT/PTT/Fibrinogen Coombs Negative Pentad in <5%

15 High-Yield Clinical Pearls

1The diagnosis is clinical — thrombocytopenia + MAHA + no other explanation = start TPE immediately. Do not wait for ADAMTS13.
2PLASMIC ≥6 = start TPE empirically while awaiting ADAMTS13. Calculate on every suspected TMA patient.
3Normal PT / PTT / fibrinogen = NOT DIC. The most tested distinction. Only platelet plug pathway is dysfunctional in TTP.
4Coombs negative = NOT AIHA. TTP is mechanical hemolysis (shearing through platelet/vWF mesh).
5Mild renal failure = TTP. Severe (Cr >3–4, dialysis) = think HUS/aHUS. Renal severity is the best clinical discriminator.
6Fluctuating neurological symptoms = classic TTP. Confused → lucid → seizes. Dynamic cerebral microthrombi.
7Troponin elevation (25–50%) is underappreciated. Myocardial microthrombi → arrhythmias, cardiomyopathy, sudden death. Always check troponin + ECG.
8Platelet transfusion is contraindicated (rare exceptions). Adding platelets = fuel to the fire. Most commonly tested management principle.
9Draw ADAMTS13 BEFORE plasma exchange. TPE normalizes ADAMTS13, making the test uninterpretable. Common exam pitfall.
10Give rituximab AFTER TPE (not before). TPE removes rituximab from plasma.
11Caplacizumab works immediately (blocks vWF-platelet). Rituximab takes weeks (B cell depletion). Complementary, not redundant.
12ADAMTS13 monitoring is lifelong. 30–50% relapse. Preemptive rituximab when ADAMTS13 <10% in remission — before clinical relapse.
13Congenital TTP: FFP infusions, NOT plasma exchange (no antibodies to remove). Board-relevant distinction.
14Pregnancy TMA: ADAMTS13 <10% = TTP, not HELLP. HELLP improves after delivery; TTP does not.
15Untreated TTP: >90% mortality — one of the highest in medicine. True hematologic emergency requiring same-day TPE.

  • Bendapudi PK, et al. PLASMIC score for predicting severe ADAMTS13 deficiency. Lancet Haematol. 2017;4(4):e157-e164.
  • ASH 2020 guidelines on management of TTP. Blood Adv. 2020;4(20):4814-4839.
  • George JN, Nester CM. Syndromes of thrombotic microangiopathy. N Engl J Med. 2014;371(7):654-666.
  • Kremer Hovinga JA, et al. Pathophysiology, diagnosis, and treatment of TTP. Blood. 2017;129(21):2836-2846.
  • Sayani FA, Abrams CS. How I treat refractory TTP. Blood. 2015;125(25):3860-3867.
